---
title: '$\Lambda$CDM Cosmological Model'
nocite: |
  @Carmona:2022pro
---

\section{Field equations}

In order to describe an homogeneous and isotropic universe like in the framework of GR, one should solve the Einstein field equations using a homogeneous, isotropic, and expanding metric (the FLRW metric),
\begin{equation}
    ds^2 = dt^2 - a^2(t) \left(\frac{dr^2}{1-k r^2}+r^2 d\Omega\right) \,,
    \label{eq:FLRW}
\end{equation}
where $k$ is the curvature and $a(t)$ the scale factor of the universe, which is closely related to the redshift, $a(t)=1/(1+z(t))$. We can use this relation to also connect the temporal coordinate to the redshift variable,
\begin{align}
    a(t) &=\frac{1}{1+z(t)} \\ 
    \frac{\dot a(t)}{a(t)} dt &= -\frac{dz}{1+z(t)} \\
    dt &= -\frac{dz}{H(z)(1+z)} \,,
    \label{eq:dtdz}
\end{align}
where we have defined the Hubble parameter[^1] as $H=\dot a/a$.

[^1]: We will not discuss here the Hubble tension problem.

\section{Friedman equations}

Using the FLRW metric, Eq. \eqref{eq:FLRW}, to solve the Einstein equations leads to the Friedmann equations, which are the basis of the standard cosmology. The first Friedmann equation can be written in terms of the cosmic density of radiation ($\Omega_r$), matter ($\Omega_m$), curvature ($\Omega_k$), and dark energy ($\Omega_\Lambda$),
\begin{gather}
    H(z) = H_0 \sqrt{\Omega_r (1+z)^4 + \Omega_m (1+z)^3 + \Omega_k (1+z)^2 + \Omega_\Lambda} \,.
    \label{eq:Hz_full}
\end{gather}
The model does not predict the values of these cosmic densities, so they should be adjusted using cosmological observations and measurements. For more information see \cite{ReyesHung:2023udr}.

\section{Mermaid map}

```{mermaid}
mindmap
  root((Wiki Home))
    Math
      Calculus
      Linear Algebra
    Physics
      Classical Mechanics
      Quantum Mechanics
        Schrödinger Eq
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

// 1. Load the D3.js library natively through Quarto
d3 = require("d3@7")

// 2. Define your Wiki structure (Add new topics here!)
data = {
  nodes: [
    {id: "Wiki Home", group: 1},
    {id: "Math", group: 2},
    {id: "Physics", group: 2},
    {id: "Calculus", group: 3},
    {id: "Quantum", group: 3}
  ],
  links: [
    {source: "Wiki Home", target: "Math"},
    {source: "Wiki Home", target: "Physics"},
    {source: "Math", target: "Calculus"},
    {source: "Physics", target: "Quantum"}
  ]
}

// 3. Build the Physics Engine and Canvas
chart = {
  const width = 800;
  const height = 500;

  const nodes = data.nodes.map(d => Object.create(d));
  const links = data.links.map(d => Object.create(d));

  // The Physics rules (adjust distance and strength to change the "bounce")
  const simulation = d3.forceSimulation(nodes)
      .force("link", d3.forceLink(links).id(d => d.id).distance(120))
      .force("charge", d3.forceManyBody().strength(-400))
      .force("center", d3.forceCenter(width / 2, height / 2));

  // The Canvas
  const svg = d3.create("svg")
      .attr("viewBox", [0, 0, width, height])
      .attr("style", "max-width: 100%; height: auto; background-color: #1a1a1a; border-radius: 8px; cursor: grab;");

  const container = svg.append("g");

  // Add Scroll-Wheel Zoom & Pan
  const zoom = d3.zoom()
      .scaleExtent([0.5, 4]) // How far out/in you can zoom
      .on("zoom", (event) => {
          container.attr("transform", event.transform);
      });
  svg.call(zoom);

  // Draw the connecting lines
  const link = container.append("g")
      .attr("stroke", "#444") // Dark grey lines
      .attr("stroke-opacity", 0.8)
    .selectAll("line")
    .data(links)
    .join("line")
      .attr("stroke-width", 2);

  // Draw the Nodes
  const node = container.append("g")
      .attr("stroke", "#1a1a1a")
      .attr("stroke-width", 1.5)
    .selectAll("circle")
    .data(nodes)
    .join("circle")
      .attr("r", 10)
      .attr("fill", d => d.group === 1 ? "#4a90e2" : "#6c7a89") // Blue for home, grey for rest
      .style("cursor", "pointer")
      .call(drag(simulation))
      // 4. THE NAVIGATION LOGIC: What happens when you click a node
      .on("click", (event, d) => {
         const urls = {
             "Math": "doc/Math/index.html",
             "Physics": "doc/Physics/index.html"
         };
         if(urls[d.id]) {
             window.location.href = urls[d.id];
         }
      });

  // Add the text labels next to the nodes
  const labels = container.append("g")
      .selectAll("text")
      .data(nodes)
      .join("text")
      .attr("dx", 15)
      .attr("dy", ".35em")
      .text(d => d.id)
      .style("fill", "#e0e0e0")
      .style("font-family", "sans-serif")
      .style("font-size", "14px")
      .style("pointer-events", "none");

  // Animate the physics each frame
  simulation.on("tick", () => {
    link.attr("x1", d => d.source.x).attr("y1", d => d.source.y)
        .attr("x2", d => d.target.x).attr("y2", d => d.target.y);
    node.attr("cx", d => d.x).attr("cy", d => d.y);
    labels.attr("x", d => d.x).attr("y", d => d.y);
  });

  return svg.node();
}

// 5. The Dragging Logic
drag = simulation => {
  function dragstarted(event, d) {
    if (!event.active) simulation.alphaTarget(0.3).restart();
    d.fx = d.x; d.fy = d.y;
  }
  function dragged(event, d) {
    d.fx = event.x; d.fy = event.y;
  }
  function dragended(event, d) {
    if (!event.active) simulation.alphaTarget(0);
    d.fx = null; d.fy = null;
  }
  return d3.drag().on("start", dragstarted).on("drag", dragged).on("end", dragended);
}
```

## List of Authors {id="authors" .appendix}

- Albert Einstein
- Isaac Newton
